title: Emergency Medicine
chapterIntro: The emergency department demands rapid assessment and clear communication in minutes, not hours. A monolingual Spanish-speaking patient arrives with chest pain and difficulty breathing—your triage nurse needs to establish vital signs, pain location, and allergy history before the provider arrives. This chapter covers the high-frequency Spanish phrases that filter patients into critical pathways and keep them safe during the most stressful moments of care.

grammarFocus.title: Interrogatives (Question Words) in High-Pressure Settings
grammarFocus.explanation: Emergency medicine relies on rapid-fire questions: ¿Dónde le duele? (Where does it hurt?) ¿Cuándo empezó? (When did it start?) ¿Tiene alergias? (Do you have allergies?). These interrogatives cut through noise and establish priority. Unlike conversational Spanish, ER Spanish uses short, direct questions without softening language—clarity and speed are paramount. The inverted question order (¿Cuánto tiempo? vs Cuánto tiempo...) signals urgency and helps bilingual patients recognize you're asking, not telling.

examples.en: Where exactly does the pain start?
On a scale of one to ten, how bad is the pain?
examples.es: En una escala del uno al diez, ¿qué tan fuerte es el dolor?

examples.en: When did this pain start—minutes, hours, or days ago?
examples.es: ¿Cuándo comenzó este dolor—hace minutos, horas, o días?

examples.en: Do you have any allergies to medications?

extendedVocab.es: motivo principal de consulta / queja principal
extendedVocab.notes: The primary reason the patient came in; recorded first in triage.

extendedVocab.es: escala de dolor / escala numérica del dolor
extendedVocab.notes: Usually 0–10; verbal descriptor scales (sin dolor, leve, moderado, severo) also used.

extendedVocab.es: dificultad para respirar / falta de aire / disnea
extendedVocab.notes: Disnea is the clinical term; both phrases understood by patients.

extendedVocab.es: dolor de pecho / dolor en el pecho / dolor torácico
extendedVocab.notes: Torácico is formal/clinical; de pecho is more common in patient language.
Always ask medication allergies separately from food/environmental.
Desmayo is colloquial; síncope is clinical.

extendedVocab.es: presión arterial / presión de la sangre
extendedVocab.notes: Patients often understand both; presión sanguínea less common.
Also: latidos (heartbeats), ritmo cardíaco (heart rate/rhythm).

extendedVocab.es: saturación de oxígeno / saturación de O2 / nivel de oxígeno
extendedVocab.notes: Non-technical patients respond to 'nivel de oxígeno en la sangre.'
Lesión is broader; golpe means blow/hit—often used for head injuries.
Mareo often conflated with nausea; clarify spinning (vértigo) if present.
Use conjugated form when asking: ¿Ha vomitado?
Temperature is thermometer reading; fiebre is the symptom.
Mareo is general dizziness; vértigo is spinning sensation.
Disfagia is clinical; dificultad para tragar is patient-friendly.

extendedVocab.es: dolor abdominal / dolor de barriga / dolor de panza
extendedVocab.notes: Barriga/panza are colloquial but understood; abdominal is formal.
Hemorragia indicates heavy/serious; sangrado is general.

extendedVocab.es: tiempo de inconsciencia / cuánto tiempo estuvo inconsciente
extendedVocab.notes: Ask: ¿Cuánto tiempo estuvo sin conocimiento?

extendedVocab.es: historial de medicamentos / medicinas que toma
extendedVocab.notes: Medicinas is more conversational than medicamentos.
Both understood; ask: ¿Ha tenido alguna operación?

phrases.en: What is the main reason you came to the hospital today?
phrases.es: ¿Cuál es el motivo principal por el que vino al hospital hoy?
phrases.context: Opening triage question; sets the complaint.

phrases.en: Point to where it hurts the most.
phrases.context: When pain is localized; patient can point if language barrier is high.

phrases.en: Is this pain sharp, dull, or pressure-like?
phrases.es: ¿Es este dolor agudo, sordo, o como presión?
phrases.context: Helps differentiate cardiac vs musculoskeletal vs other causes.

phrases.en: Did this pain come on suddenly or gradually?
phrases.es: ¿Comenzó el dolor de repente o gradualmente?
phrases.context: Sudden onset suggests acute event; gradual onset may be chronic exacerbation.

phrases.en: Are you having trouble breathing right now?
Have you had any recent injuries or accidents?
phrases.es: ¿Ha tenido alguna lesión o accidente recientemente?
phrases.context: Trauma history; often missed in language barrier situations.

phrases.en: Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
When did you last eat or drink anything?
phrases.es: ¿Cuándo fue la última vez que comió o bebió algo?
phrases.context: NPO assessment; critical pre-intubation and pre-procedure.

phrases.en: Are you on any medications at home?
phrases.context: Medication history; may reveal contraindications or drug interactions.

phrases.en: Have you had this problem before?
phrases.context: Establishes if acute or chronic exacerbation.

phrases.en: Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
phrases.es: ¿Puede seguir mi dedo con los ojos?
phrases.context: Neuro check; doesn't require patient to speak.

phrases.en: Squeeze my hands and push your feet against me.
phrases.es: Apriete mis manos y empuje sus pies contra mí.

exercises.title: Triage Intake—Fill in the Blank
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct Spanish term from the word bank.

items.prompt: En una escala del uno al diez, ¿qué tan fuerte es su _____?
¿Ha tenido un _____ o pérdida del conocimiento?

exercises.title: Quick Translation—Common ER Phrases
exercises.instructions: Translate each English phrase into Spanish. Focus on clarity and directness.

items.en: I need to check your vital signs.
Stay calm; you're in the emergency room.
items.es: Manténgase tranquilo; está en la sala de emergencias.

items.en: Let me listen to your heart and lungs.
items.es: Déjeme escuchar su corazón y pulmones.

items.en: The doctor will see you shortly.
items.es: El doctor lo verá en breve.

culturalNote: Spanish-speaking patients in US emergency departments may come from diverse regions—Mexico, Central America, Caribbean, and South America each have distinct medical vocabulary. For example, toma (take) versus coge (grab) regional differences, and the word for "straw" changes meaning geographically in ways that seem unrelated but index cultural familiarity. In ER settings, default to high-frequency, non-regional terms: dolor (not mal), medicamento (not remedio, which some regions use for home remedy), and fiebre (universal). If a patient uses regional slang (barriga for abdomen, panza for belly), mirror it back—it signals respect and speeds rapport. Additionally, many monolingual ER patients have never been to a US hospital; they may not understand hospital hierarchy or triage logic. Explaining ¿Por qué espero si es una emergencia? (Why do I wait if it's an emergency?) with a brief statement about acuity sorting (Estamos reviendo a los pacientes más graves primero) defuses frustration and builds trust during high-stress moments.
